NKJV.1: My brethren, let not many of you become teachers, knowing that we shall receive a stricter judgment.
NKJV.2: For we all stumble in many things. If anyone does not stumble in word, he is a perfect man, able also to bridle the whole body.
NKJV.3: Indeed, we put bits in horses’ mouths that they may obey us, and we turn their whole body.
NKJV.4: Look also at ships: although they are so large and are driven by fierce winds, they are turned by a very small rudder wherever the pilot desires.
NKJV.5: Even so the tongue is a little member and boasts great things. See how great a forest a little fire kindles!
NKJV.6: And the tongue is a fire, a world of iniquity. The tongue is so set among our members that it defiles the whole body, and sets on fire the course of nature; and it is set on fire by hell.
NKJV.7: For every kind of beast and bird, of reptile and creature of the sea, is tamed and has been tamed by mankind.
NKJV.8: But no man can tame the tongue. It is an unruly evil, full of deadly poison.
NKJV.9: With it we bless our God and Father, and with it we curse men, who have been made in the similitude of God.
NKJV.10: Out of the same mouth proceed blessing and cursing. My brethren, these things ought not to be so.
NKJV.11: Does a spring send forth fresh water and bitter from the same opening?
NKJV.12: Can a fig tree, my brethren, bear olives, or a grapevine bear figs? Thus no spring yields both salt water and fresh.
NKJV.13: Who is wise and understanding among you? Let him show by good conduct that his works are done in the meekness of wisdom.
NKJV.14: But if you have bitter envy and self-seeking in your hearts, do not boast and lie against the truth.
NKJV.15: This wisdom does not descend from above, but is earthly, sensual, demonic.
NKJV.16: For where envy and self-seeking exist, confusion and every evil thing are there.
NKJV.17: But the wisdom that is from above is first pure, then peaceable, gentle, willing to yield, full of mercy and good fruits, without partiality and without hypocrisy.
NKJV.18: Now the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace by those who make peace.

ESV.1: Not many of you should become teachers, my brothers, for you know that we who teach will be judged with greater strictness.
ESV.2: For we all stumble in many ways. And if anyone does not stumble in what he says, he is a perfect man, able also to bridle his whole body.
ESV.3: If we put bits into the mouths of horses so that they obey us, we guide their whole bodies as well.
ESV.4: Look at the ships also: though they are so large and are driven by strong winds, they are guided by a very small rudder wherever the will of the pilot directs.
ESV.5: So also the tongue is a small member, yet it boasts of great things. How great a forest is set ablaze by such a small fire!
ESV.6: And the tongue is a fire, a world of unrighteousness. The tongue is set among our members, staining the whole body, setting on fire the entire course of life,  and set on fire by hell.
ESV.7: For every kind of beast and bird, of reptile and sea creature, can be tamed and has been tamed by mankind,
ESV.8: but no human being can tame the tongue. It is a restless evil, full of deadly poison.
ESV.9: With it we bless our Lord and Father, and with it we curse people who are made in the likeness of God.
ESV.10: From the same mouth come blessing and cursing. My brothers,  these things ought not to be so.
ESV.11: Does a spring pour forth from the same opening both fresh and salt water?
ESV.12: Can a fig tree, my brothers, bear olives, or a grapevine produce figs? Neither can a salt pond yield fresh water.
ESV.13: Who is wise and understanding among you? By his good conduct let him show his works in the meekness of wisdom.
ESV.14: But if you have bitter jealousy and selfish ambition in your hearts, do not boast and be false to the truth.
ESV.15: This is not the wisdom that comes down from above, but is earthly, unspiritual, demonic.
ESV.16: For where jealousy and selfish ambition exist, there will be disorder and every vile practice.
ESV.17: But the wisdom from above is first pure, then peaceable, gentle, open to reason, full of mercy and good fruits, impartial and sincere.
ESV.18: And a harvest of righteousness is sown in peace by those who make peace.

NASB.1: Let not many [of you] become teachers, my brethren, knowing that as such we will incur a stricter judgment.
NASB.2: For we all stumble in many [ways]. If anyone does not stumble in what he says, he is a perfect man, able to bridle the whole body as well.
NASB.3: Now if we put the bits into the horses' mouths so that they will obey us, we direct their entire body as well.
NASB.4: Look at the ships also, though they are so great and are driven by strong winds, are still directed by a very small rudder wherever the inclination of the pilot desires.
NASB.5: So also the tongue is a small part of the body, and [yet] it boasts of great things. See how great a forest is set aflame by such a small fire!
NASB.6: And the tongue is a fire, the [very] world of iniquity; the tongue is set among our members as that which defiles the entire body, and sets on fire the course of [our] life, and is set on fire by hell.
NASB.7: For every species of beasts and birds, of reptiles and creatures of the sea, is tamed and has been tamed by the human race.
NASB.8: But no one can tame the tongue; [it is] a restless evil [and] full of deadly poison.
NASB.9: With it we bless [our] Lord and Father, and with it we curse men, who have been made in the likeness of God;
NASB.10: from the same mouth come [both] blessing and cursing. My brethren, these things ought not to be this way.
NASB.11: Does a fountain send out from the same opening [both] fresh and bitter [water]?
NASB.12: Can a fig tree, my brethren, produce olives, or a vine produce figs? Nor [can] salt water produce fresh.
NASB.13: Who among you is wise and understanding? Let him show by his good behavior his deeds in the gentleness of wisdom.
NASB.14: But if you have bitter jealousy and selfish ambition in your heart, do not be arrogant and [so] lie against the truth.
NASB.15: This wisdom is not that which comes down from above, but is earthly, natural, demonic.
NASB.16: For where jealousy and selfish ambition exist, there is disorder and every evil thing.
NASB.17: But the wisdom from above is first pure, then peaceable, gentle, reasonable, full of mercy and good fruits, unwavering, without hypocrisy.
NASB.18: And the seed whose fruit is righteousness is sown in peace by those who make peace.

NLT.1: Dear brothers and sisters, not many of you should become teachers in the church, for we who teach will be judged more strictly.
NLT.2: Indeed, we all make many mistakes. For if we could control our tongues, we would be perfect and could also control ourselves in every other way.
NLT.3: We can make a large horse go wherever we want by means of a small bit in its mouth.
NLT.4: And a small rudder makes a huge ship turn wherever the pilot chooses to go, even though the winds are strong.
NLT.5: In the same way, the tongue is a small thing that makes grand speeches. But a tiny spark can set a great forest on fire.
NLT.6: And among all the parts of the body, the tongue is a flame of fire. It is a whole world of wickedness, corrupting your entire body. It can set your whole life on fire, for it is set on fire by hell itself.
NLT.7: People can tame all kinds of animals, birds, reptiles, and fish,
NLT.8: but no one can tame the tongue. It is restless and evil, full of deadly poison.
NLT.9: Sometimes it praises our Lord and Father, and sometimes it curses those who have been made in the image of God.
NLT.10: And so blessing and cursing come pouring out of the same mouth. Surely, my brothers and sisters, this is not right!
NLT.11: Does a spring of water bubble out with both fresh water and bitter water?
NLT.12: Does a fig tree produce olives, or a grapevine produce figs? No, and you can’t draw fresh water from a salty spring.
NLT.13: If you are wise and understand God’s ways, prove it by living an honorable life, doing good works with the humility that comes from wisdom.
NLT.14: But if you are bitterly jealous and there is selfish ambition in your heart, don’t cover up the truth with boasting and lying.
NLT.15: For jealousy and selfishness are not God’s kind of wisdom. Such things are earthly, unspiritual, and demonic.
NLT.16: For wherever there is jealousy and selfish ambition, there you will find disorder and evil of every kind.
NLT.17: But the wisdom from above is first of all pure. It is also peace loving, gentle at all times, and willing to yield to others. It is full of mercy and the fruit of good deeds. It shows no favoritism and is always sincere.
NLT.18: And those who are peacemakers will plant seeds of peace and reap a harvest of righteousness.

WEB.1: Let not many of you be teachers, my brothers, knowing that we will receive heavier judgment.
WEB.2: For in many things we all stumble. If anyone doesn’t stumble in word, the same is a perfect man, able to bridle the whole body also.
WEB.3: Indeed, we put bits into the horses’ mouths so that they may obey us, and we guide their whole body.
WEB.4: Behold,  the ships also, though they are so big and are driven by fierce winds, are yet guided by a very small rudder, wherever the pilot desires.
WEB.5: So the tongue is also a little member, and boasts great things. See how a small fire can spread to a large forest!
WEB.6: And the tongue is a fire. The world of iniquity among our members is the tongue, which defiles the whole body, and sets on fire the course of nature, and is set on fire by Gehenna.
WEB.7: For every kind of animal, bird, creeping thing, and sea creature, is tamed, and has been tamed by mankind;
WEB.8: but nobody can tame the tongue. It is a restless evil, full of deadly poison.
WEB.9: With it we bless our God and Father, and with it we curse men, who are made in the image of God.
WEB.10: Out of the same mouth comes blessing and cursing. My brothers, these things ought not to be so.
WEB.11: Does a spring send out from the same opening fresh and bitter water?
WEB.12: Can a fig tree, my brothers, yield olives, or a vine figs? Thus no spring yields both salt water and fresh water.
WEB.13: Who is wise and understanding among you? Let him show by his good conduct that his deeds are done in gentleness of wisdom.
WEB.14: But if you have bitter jealousy and selfish ambition in your heart, don’t boast and don’t lie against the truth.
WEB.15: This wisdom is not that which comes down from above, but is earthly, sensual, and demonic.
WEB.16: For where jealousy and selfish ambition are, there is confusion and every evil deed.
WEB.17: But the wisdom that is from above is first pure, then peaceful, gentle, reasonable, full of mercy and good fruits, without partiality, and without hypocrisy.
WEB.18: Now the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace by those who make peace.

CSB17.1: Not many should become teachers, my brothers, <sup>[1]</sup> because you know that we will receive a stricter judgment. <sup>ⓜ</sup>
CSB17.2: For we all stumble in many ways. <sup>ⓝ</sup> If anyone does not stumble in what he says, he is mature, able also to control the whole body. <sup>ⓐ</sup>
CSB17.3: Now if we put bits into the mouths of horses so that they obey us, <sup>ⓑ</sup> we direct their whole bodies.
CSB17.4: And consider ships: Though very large and driven by fierce winds, they are guided by a very small rudder wherever the will of the pilot directs.
CSB17.5: So too, though the tongue is a small part of the body, it boasts great things. <sup>ⓒ</sup> Consider how a small fire sets ablaze a large forest.
CSB17.6: And the tongue is a fire. The tongue, a world of unrighteousness, is placed <sup>[2]</sup> among our members. It stains the whole body, <sup>ⓓ</sup> sets the course of life on fire, and is itself set on fire by hell.
CSB17.7: Every kind of animal, bird, reptile, and fish is tamed and has been tamed by humankind,
CSB17.8: but no one can tame the tongue. It is a restless evil, full of deadly poison. <sup>ⓔ</sup>
CSB17.9: With the tongue we bless our Lord and Father, and with it we curse people who are made in God’s likeness. <sup>ⓕ</sup>
CSB17.10: Blessing and cursing come out of the same mouth. My brothers and sisters, these things should not be this way.
CSB17.11: Does a spring pour out sweet and bitter water from the same opening?
CSB17.12: Can a fig tree produce olives, my brothers and sisters, or a grapevine produce figs? Neither can a saltwater spring yield fresh water.
CSB17.13: Who among you is wise and understanding? By his good conduct he should show that his works are done in the gentleness that comes from wisdom. <sup>ⓖ</sup>
CSB17.14: But if you have bitter envy and selfish ambition in your heart, don’t boast and deny the truth. <sup>ⓗ</sup>
CSB17.15: Such wisdom does not come down from above but is earthly, unspiritual, demonic. <sup>ⓘ</sup>
CSB17.16: For where there is envy and selfish ambition, there is disorder and every evil practice.
CSB17.17: But the wisdom from above is first pure, then peace-loving, gentle, compliant, full of mercy and good fruits, unwavering, without pretense. <sup>ⓙ</sup>
CSB17.18: And the fruit of righteousness <sup>ⓚ</sup> is sown in peace by those who cultivate peace.

AMP.1: Not many [of you] should become teachers [serving in an official teaching capacity], my brothers and sisters, for you know that we [who are teachers] will be judged by a higher standard [because we have assumed greater accountability and more condemnation if we teach incorrectly].
AMP.2: For we all stumble and sin in many ways. If anyone does not stumble in what he says [never saying the wrong thing], he is a perfect man [fully developed in character, without serious flaws], able to bridle his whole body and rein in his entire nature [taming his human faults and weaknesses].
AMP.3: Now if we put bits into the horses' mouths to make them obey us, we guide their whole body as well.
AMP.4: And look at the ships. Even though they are so large and are driven by strong winds, they are still directed by a very small rudder wherever the impulse of the helmsman determines.
AMP.5:
In the same sense, the tongue is a small part of the body, and yet it boasts of great things.
See [by comparison] how great a forest is set on fire by a small spark!
AMP.6: And the tongue is [in a sense] a fire, the very world of injustice and unrighteousness; the tongue is set among our members as that which contaminates the entire body, and sets on fire the course of our life [the cycle of man's existence], and is itself set on fire by hell (Gehenna).
AMP.7: For every species of beasts and birds, of reptiles and sea creatures, is tamed and has been tamed by the human race.
AMP.8: But no one can tame the human tongue; it is a restless evil [undisciplined, unstable], full of deadly poison.
AMP.9: With it we bless our Lord and Father, and with it we curse men, who have been made in the likeness of God.
AMP.10: Out of the same mouth come both blessing and cursing. These things, my brothers, should not be this way [for we have a moral obligation to speak in a manner that reflects our fear of God and profound respect for His precepts].
AMP.11: Does a spring send out from the same opening both fresh and bitter water?
AMP.12: Can a fig tree, my brothers, produce olives, or a grapevine produce figs? Nor can salt water produce fresh.
AMP.13: Who among you is wise and intelligent? Let him by his good conduct show his [good] deeds with the gentleness and humility of true wisdom.
AMP.14: But if you have bitter jealousy and selfish ambition in your hearts, do not be arrogant, and [as a result] be in defiance of the truth.
AMP.15: This [superficial] wisdom is not that which comes down from above, but is earthly (secular), natural (unspiritual), even demonic.
AMP.16: For where jealousy and selfish ambition exist, there is disorder [unrest, rebellion] and every evil thing and morally degrading practice.
AMP.17: But the wisdom from above is first pure [morally and spiritually undefiled], then peace-loving [courteous, considerate], gentle, reasonable [and willing to listen], full of compassion and good fruits. It is unwavering, without [self-righteous] hypocrisy [and self-serving guile].
AMP.18: And the seed whose fruit is righteousness (spiritual maturity) is sown in peace by those who make peace [by actively encouraging goodwill between individuals].

MSG.1: Don't be in any rush to become a teacher, my friends. Teaching is highly responsible work. Teachers are held to the strictest standards.
MSG.2: And none of us is perfectly qualified. We get it wrong nearly every time we open our mouths. If you could find someone whose speech was perfectly true, you'd have a perfect person, in perfect control of life.
MSG.3: A bit in the mouth of a horse controls the whole horse.
MSG.4: A small rudder on a huge ship in the hands of a skilled captain sets a course in the face of the strongest winds.
MSG.5: A word out of your mouth may seem of no account, but it can accomplish nearly anything—or destroy it! It only takes a spark, remember, to set off a forest fire.
MSG.6: A careless or wrongly placed word out of your mouth can do that. By our speech we can ruin the world, turn harmony to chaos, throw mud on a reputation, send the whole world up in smoke and go up in smoke with it, smoke right from the pit of hell.
MSG.7: This is scary: You can tame a tiger,
MSG.8: but you can't tame a tongue—it's never been done. The tongue runs wild, a wanton killer.
MSG.9: With our tongues we bless God our Father; with the same tongues we curse the very men and women he made in his image.
MSG.10: Curses and blessings out of the same mouth! My friends, this can't go on.
MSG.11: A spring doesn't gush fresh water one day and brackish the next, does it?
MSG.12: Apple trees don't bear strawberries, do they? Raspberry bushes don't bear apples, do they? You're not going to dip into a polluted mud hole and get a cup of clear, cool water, are you?
MSG.13: Do you want to be counted wise, to build a reputation for wisdom? Here's what you do: Live well, live wisely, live humbly. It's the way you live, not the way you talk, that counts.
MSG.14: Mean-spirited ambition isn't wisdom. Boasting that you are wise isn't wisdom. Twisting the truth to make yourselves sound wise isn't wisdom.
MSG.15: It's the furthest thing from wisdom—it's animal cunning, devilish conniving.
MSG.16: Whenever you're trying to look better than others or get the better of others, things fall apart and everyone ends up at the others' throats.
MSG.17: Real wisdom, God's wisdom, begins with a holy life and is characterized by getting along with others. It is gentle and reasonable, overflowing with mercy and blessings, not hot one day and cold the next, not two-faced.
MSG.18: You can develop a healthy, robust community that lives right with God and enjoy its results only if you do the hard work of getting along with each other, treating each other with dignity and honor.

NIV.1:
Taming the Tongue
Not many of you should presume to be teachers, my brothers, because you know that we who teach will be judged more strictly.
NIV.2: We all stumble in many ways. If anyone is never at fault in what he says, he is a perfect man, able to keep his whole body in check.
NIV.3: When we put bits into the mouths of horses to make them obey us, we can turn the whole animal.
NIV.4: Or take ships as an example. Although they are so large and are driven by strong winds, they are steered by a very small rudder wherever the pilot wants to go.
NIV.5: Likewise the tongue is a small part of the body, but it makes great boasts. Consider what a great forest is set on fire by a small spark.
NIV.6: The tongue also is a fire, a world of evil among the parts of the body. It corrupts the whole person, sets the whole course of his life on fire, and is itself set on fire by hell.
NIV.7: All kinds of animals, birds, reptiles and creatures of the sea are being tamed and have been tamed by man,
NIV.8: but no man can tame the tongue. It is a restless evil, full of deadly poison.
NIV.9: With the tongue we praise our Lord and Father, and with it we curse men, who have been made in God’s likeness.
NIV.10: Out of the same mouth come praise and cursing. My brothers, this should not be.
NIV.11: Can both fresh water and salt water flow from the same spring?
NIV.12: My brothers, can a fig tree bear olives, or a grapevine bear figs? Neither can a salt spring produce fresh water.
NIV.13:
Two Kinds of Wisdom
Who is wise and understanding among you? Let him show it by his good life, by deeds done in the humility that comes from wisdom.
NIV.14: But if you harbor bitter envy and selfish ambition in your hearts, do not boast about it or deny the truth.
NIV.15: Such “wisdom” does not come down from heaven but is earthly, unspiritual, of the devil.
NIV.16: For where you have envy and selfish ambition, there you find disorder and every evil practice.
NIV.17: But the wisdom that comes from heaven is first of all pure; then peace-loving, considerate, submissive, full of mercy and good fruit, impartial and sincere.
NIV.18: Peacemakers who sow in peace raise a harvest of righteousness.

NRSVCE.1: Not many of you should become teachers, my brothers and sisters, for you know that we who teach will be judged with greater strictness.
NRSVCE.2: For all of us make many mistakes. Anyone who makes no mistakes in speaking is perfect, able to keep the whole body in check with a bridle.
NRSVCE.3: If we put bits into the mouths of horses to make them obey us, we guide their whole bodies.
NRSVCE.4: Or look at ships: though they are so large that it takes strong winds to drive them, yet they are guided by a very small rudder wherever the will of the pilot directs.
NRSVCE.5: So also the tongue is a small member, yet it boasts of great exploits. How great a forest is set ablaze by a small fire!
NRSVCE.6: And the tongue is a fire. The tongue is placed among our members as a world of iniquity; it stains the whole body, sets on fire the cycle of nature, and is itself set on fire by hell.
NRSVCE.7: For every species of beast and bird, of reptile and sea creature, can be tamed and has been tamed by the human species,
NRSVCE.8: but no one can tame the tongue — a restless evil, full of deadly poison.
NRSVCE.9: With it we bless the Lord and Father, and with it we curse those who are made in the likeness of God.
NRSVCE.10: From the same mouth come blessing and cursing. My brothers and sisters, this ought not to be so.
NRSVCE.11: Does a spring pour forth from the same opening both fresh and brackish water?
NRSVCE.12: Can a fig tree, my brothers and sisters, yield olives, or a grapevine figs? No more can salt water yield fresh.
NRSVCE.13: Who is wise and understanding among you? Show by your good life that your works are done with gentleness born of wisdom.
NRSVCE.14: But if you have bitter envy and selfish ambition in your hearts, do not be boastful and false to the truth.
NRSVCE.15: Such wisdom does not come down from above, but is earthly, unspiritual, devilish.
NRSVCE.16: For where there is envy and selfish ambition, there will also be disorder and wickedness of every kind.
NRSVCE.17: But the wisdom from above is first pure, then peaceable, gentle, willing to yield, full of mercy and good fruits, without a trace of partiality or hypocrisy.
NRSVCE.18: And a harvest of righteousness is sown in peace for those who make peace.

NET.1: Not many of you should become teachers, my brothers and sisters, because you know that we will be judged more strictly.
NET.2: For we all stumble in many ways. If someone does not stumble in what he says, he is a perfect individual, able to control the entire body as well.
NET.3: And if we put bits into the mouths of horses to get them to obey us, then we guide their entire bodies.
NET.4: Look at ships too: Though they are so large and driven by harsh winds, they are steered by a tiny rudder wherever the pilot’s inclination directs.
NET.5: So too the tongue is a small part of the body, yet it has great pretensions. Think how small a flame sets a huge forest ablaze.
NET.6: And the tongue is a fire! The tongue represents the world of wrongdoing among the parts of our bodies. It pollutes the entire body and sets fire to the course of human existence — and is set on fire by hell.
NET.7: For every kind of animal, bird, reptile, and sea creature is subdued and has been subdued by humankind.
NET.8: But no human being can subdue the tongue; it is a restless evil, full of deadly poison.
NET.9: With it we bless the Lord and Father, and with it we curse people made in God’s image.
NET.10: From the same mouth come blessing and cursing. These things should not be so, my brothers and sisters.
NET.11: A spring does not pour out fresh water and bitter water from the same opening, does it?
NET.12: Can a fig tree produce olives, my brothers and sisters, or a vine produce figs? Neither can a salt water spring produce fresh water.
NET.13: Who is wise and understanding among you? By his good conduct he should show his works done in the gentleness that wisdom brings.
NET.14: But if you have bitter jealousy and selfishness in your hearts, do not boast and tell lies against the truth.
NET.15: Such wisdom does not come from above but is earthly, natural, demonic.
NET.16: For where there is jealousy and selfishness, there is disorder and every evil practice.
NET.17: But the wisdom from above is first pure, then peaceable, gentle, accommodating, full of mercy and good fruit, impartial, and not hypocritical.
NET.18: And the fruit that consists of righteousness is planted in peace among those who make peace.
